windows.transcript: but that's basically it on why a candle will extinguish itself. The flame is still as big as it would be as if it was in a jar that was meant for it
windows.embed_text_v2: Explaining candle burning with hands gesturing over a chalk diagram of a glass candle jar cross-section showing wick, flame and cream wax drawn on a black surface, Standley watermark, educational illustration.
windows.summary_v2: An explainer clip where two hands gesture and point over a chalk drawing of a candle-jar cross-section, illustrating the wick, flame and wax while the narrator discusses candle burning.

tags_v2.setting: chalk drawing on black surface, overhead

seconds_v2.desc: Overhead view of a chalk drawing on a black surface depicting a glass candle jar cross-section with cream-colored wax and a central wick and flame; two hands rest at the top edges of the diagram. 'Standley' watermark in the lower-left corner.
Both hands frame the top of the chalk candle-jar diagram, fingers near the drawn jar rim while the narrator explains the flame and wax illustration.
Hands spread to either side of the chalk drawing, gesturing toward the wick and flame sketched inside the jar of cream wax.
The right hand moves inward over the diagram, pointing near the drawn flame and wick at the top of the wax pool.
Right hand lifts away to the upper right while the left hand stays at the jar rim, both gesturing over the chalk candle illustration.
Hands return to frame the chalk candle-jar drawing, indicating the flame and the layered wax inside the glass.
Right hand hovers over the top of the diagram near the flame; the cream-colored wax and wick of the drawn jar remain centered.
Both hands rest at the sides of the chalk drawing of the candle in its jar, the flame and wick clearly sketched at center.
Hands settle at the top corners of the chalk candle-jar diagram, fingers near the drawn rim, the wax-filled jar and flame fully visible.
